title: Dehumidifying Coil Rating Parameters
summary: Standard rating parameters for dehumidifying coils per AHRI Standard 410.
evidence_quote:
Entering air dry-bulb temperature: 65 to 100°F
Entering air wet-bulb temperature: 60 to 85°F
Air face velocity: 200 to 800 fpm
Evaporator refrigerant saturation temperature: 30 to 55°F
Entering chilled-water temperature: 35 to 65°F
Water velocity: 1 to 8 fps

title: Glycol Solution Rating Parameters
summary: Rating parameters for ethylene glycol solution coils.
evidence_quote: For ethylene glycol solution: 1 to 6 fps, 0 to 90°F entering dry-bulb temperature, 60 to 80°F entering wet-bulb temperature, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by weight

title: Standard Rating Airflow for Unitary Equipment
summary: Standard airflow rating point for unitary equipment is 33.4 cfm per 1000 Btu/h (400 cfm per ton), not to exceed 37.5 cfm per 1000 Btu/h.
evidence_quote: 33.4 cfm per 1000 Btu/h (or 400 cfm per ton of refrigeration effect), not to exceed 37.5 cfm per 100 Btu/h for unitary equipment.

title: Standard Water Coil Operating Conditions
summary: Standard water coil circuitry operates at 4 fps, 42°F inlet water, 12°F rise (2 gpm per ton).
evidence_quote: For water coils, circuitry would operate at 4 fps, 42°F inlet water, 12°F rise (or 2 gpm per ton of refrigeration effect).

title: Standard Entering Air Conditions for Comfort Operations
summary: Standard entering air conditions for comfort operations are 80°F db and 67°F wb.
evidence_quote: The standard ratings at 80°F db and 67°F wb are representative of the entering air conditions encountered in many comfort operations.

title: Airflow Rating Standard Air Density
summary: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 0.075 lb/ft³ at 70°F and 29.92 in. Hg.
evidence_quote: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 0.075 lb/ft³ at 70°F and a barometric pressure of 29.92 in. Hg.

title: Sensible Heat Ratio Range for Dehumidifying Coils
summary: The air-side sensible heat ratio for dehumidifying coils varies from about 0.6 to 1.0.
evidence_quote: The air-side ratio of sensible to total heat removed by dehumidifying coils varies in practice from about 0.6 to 1.0

title: Typical Air Face Velocity for Comfort Dehumidifying Coils
summary: Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 400 to 500 fpm air face velocity.
evidence_quote: Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 400 to 500 fpm air face velocity.

title: Air Friction Reduction with Hydrophilic Coating
summary: Hydrophilic coatings can reduce air pressure drop across a coil by up to 30% compared to new untreated coil.
evidence_quote: Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil.

title: Dry Coil Airflow Resistance vs. Dehumidifying Coil
summary: A completely dry coil offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil.
evidence_quote: A completely dry coil, removing only sensible heat, offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil removing both sensible and latent heat.

title: Freezing Risk for Dehumidifying Coils
summary: Freezing occurs when coil surface temperature falls below 32°F; typically not with standard coils unless refrigerant evaporating temperature at coil outlet is below 25 to 28°F saturated.
evidence_quote: Freezing occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature falls below 32°F. Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below 25 to 28°F saturated

title: Light-Load Dehumidification in Humid Climates
summary: In hot, humid climates, light-load conditions result in higher dew points unless special means like reheat or dual path are used.
evidence_quote: In hot, humid climates, the light-load condition has a higher proportion of moisture and a correspondingly lower proportion of sensible heat. The result is higher dew points in the conditioned spaces during light-load conditions unless a special means for controlling inside dew points (e.g., reheat or dual path) is used.

title: Dual-Path Air Process for Humid Areas
summary: Dual-path air process uses a separate cooling coil to dehumidify ventilation air before mixing with recirculated air.
evidence_quote: One such refinement, the dual-path air process, uses a separate cooling coil to cool and dehumidify ventilation air before mixing it with recirculated air.

title: Maximum Coil Air Face Velocity to Prevent Water Carryover
summary: The maximum coil air face velocity should be limited to prevent water carryover into air ductwork.
evidence_quote: The maximum coil air face velocity should be limited to a value that prevents water carryover into the air ductwork.

title: Fin Surface Freezing Avoidance
summary: Fin surface freezing at light loads should be avoided; occurs when coil surface temperature falls below 32°F.
evidence_quote: Fin surface freezing at light loads should be avoided. Freezing occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature falls below 32°F.
